text: The only thing we have to fear is fear itself — nameless, unreasoning, unjustified terror which paralyzes needed efforts to convert retreat into advance.
context: Delivered at the depth of the Great Depression; the most famous line of his presidency.

text: I pledge you, I pledge myself, to a new deal for the American people.
context: Introducing the phrase and philosophy that would define his presidency.

text: We have always known that heedless self-interest was bad morals; we know now that it is bad economics.
context: Arguing that social justice and economic recovery were inseparable.

text: This great nation will endure as it has endured, will revive and will prosper. So, first of all, let me assert my firm belief that the only thing we have to fear is fear itself.
context: Reassuring a frightened nation while acknowledging the severity of the crisis.

text: I see one-third of a nation ill-housed, ill-clad, ill-nourished.
context: Famous description of the Americans still left behind by the Depression.

text: The test of our progress is not whether we add more to the abundance of those who have much; it is whether we provide enough for those who have too little.
context: Defining the moral measure of national progress.

text: Yesterday, December 7, 1941 — a date which will live in infamy — the United States of America was suddenly and deliberately attacked by naval and air forces of the Empire of Japan.
speech: Address to Congress requesting declaration of war
context: Requesting a declaration of war the day after Pearl Harbor.

text: We look forward to a world founded upon four essential human freedoms. The first is freedom of speech and expression — everywhere in the world. The second is freedom of every person to worship God in his own way — everywhere in the world. The third is freedom from want... The fourth is freedom from fear.
speech: State of the Union Address (Four Freedoms)
context: Defining the war aims in universal human terms before U.S. entry into WWII.

text: The only limit to our realization of tomorrow will be our doubts of today. Let us move forward with strong and active faith.
source: Undelivered Jefferson Day address (written the day before his death)
context: Final written words, discovered after his death.

text: We must be the great arsenal of democracy. For us this is an emergency as serious as war itself. We must apply ourselves to our task with the same resolution, the same sense of urgency, the same spirit of patriotism and sacrifice as we would show were we at war.
context: Calling for Lend-Lease aid to Britain while America was still officially neutral.

text: Repetition does not transform a lie into a truth.
context: Pushing back against propaganda and economic defeatism.

text: No man can tame a tiger into a kitten by stroking it. There can be no appeasement with ruthlessness. There can be no reasoning with an incendiary bomb.
context: Rejecting appeasement of the Axis powers.

text: We must especially beware of that small group of selfish men who would clip the wings of the American eagle in order to feather their own nests.
context: Warning against profiteers and isolationists who would weaken the national defense.

text: I have seen war. I have seen war on land and sea. I have seen blood running from the wounded. I have seen men coughing out their gassed lungs. I have seen the dead in the mud. I have seen cities destroyed. I have seen children starving. I have seen the agony of mothers and wives. I hate war.
context: Personal testimony against war from a leader who had seen WWI.

text: The nation that destroys its soil destroys itself. Forests are the lungs of our land, purifying the air and giving fresh strength to our people.
context: Linking environmental stewardship to national survival (New Deal conservation era).

text: Happiness lies not in the mere possession of money; it lies in the joy of achievement, in the thrill of creative effort.
context: Rejecting materialism as the measure of national or personal success.

text: We have learned that we cannot live alone, at peace; that our own well-being is dependent on the well-being of other nations far away.
context: Recognizing American interdependence with the world as WWII neared its end.

text: The structure of world peace cannot be the work of one man, or one party, or one nation. It must be a peace which rests on the cooperative effort of the whole world.
context: Vision for postwar international cooperation (United Nations).

text: Men are not prisoners of fate, but only prisoners of their own minds.
context: Optimistic belief in human agency to shape history.

text: The test of our democracy is not whether we can prevent a depression, but whether we can prevent a depression from becoming a catastrophe.
context: On the responsibility of government in economic crisis.
